originalTitle: 1996: Dolly the sheep, the first mammal cloned from an adult cell, is born

A1.title: The first cloned sheep
A1.text: On this day in 1996, a very special animal was born. It was a sheep. Her name was Dolly. Dolly was not a normal sheep. She had no father and no real mother. Scientists made her from one cell of another sheep. So Dolly was a copy. This is called a clone. It happened in Scotland. Many people were surprised. Some were happy. Some were worried. Dolly lived for six years. She had lambs too. Today we still talk about her.

vocabulary.definition: a farm animal with soft white hair
when a baby starts its life
a person who studies nature
the smallest part of a living thing
a thing that is the same as another thing
not happy because you think something bad can happen

A2.title: Dolly, a copy of another sheep
A2.text: On this day in 1996, scientists in Scotland made history. They created a sheep called Dolly. Dolly was the first mammal cloned from an adult cell. This means she was a copy of another, older sheep. Normally, a baby animal gets things from a mother and a father. But Dolly came from just one cell of one adult sheep. The scientists worked at the Roslin Institute, near Edinburgh. Their work was very difficult, and they tried many times before it worked. When people heard the news, they were amazed. Some thought it was wonderful for science and medicine. Others were afraid of what cloning could mean. Dolly lived a fairly normal life and had six lambs. She died in 2003.

vocabulary.definition: to make something new
an animal that feeds its babies with milk
to make an exact copy of a living thing
a grown-up person or animal, not a baby
very surprised
feeling fear

B1.title: The day science cloned a sheep
B1.text: On this day in 1996, a lamb was born in Scotland that would soon become world famous. Her name was Dolly, and she was the first mammal ever cloned from an adult body cell. In simple terms, Dolly was a living copy of another, older sheep. Normally a new animal is created from two parents, but the scientists at the Roslin Institute, near Edinburgh, took a single cell from one adult sheep and used it to grow a genetically identical animal. The process was extremely difficult: it took hundreds of attempts before a healthy lamb was finally born. When Dolly's existence was announced to the public in 1997, she caused a sensation around the world. Many people were excited about what cloning might mean for medicine and science. Others worried about where this new power might lead, and whether humans might one day be cloned too. Dolly lived for six years and gave birth to several lambs of her own before she died in 2003. Her body is still on display in a museum in Edinburgh.

vocabulary.definition: a baby sheep
having exactly the same genes
a try at doing something
to tell people something officially
something that causes great excitement
put in a place where people can see it

B2.title: Dolly the sheep and the dawn of cloning
B2.text: On this day in 1996, at the Roslin Institute near Edinburgh, a lamb was born who would change how the world thought about biology. She was named Dolly, and she was the first mammal ever cloned from an adult somatic cell — an ordinary body cell rather than a reproductive one. Instead of being created from the genes of two parents, Dolly was grown from a single cell taken from the udder of an adult ewe, making her a genetic copy of that animal. The achievement was anything but easy: the technique, known as somatic cell nuclear transfer, succeeded only after 277 attempts. Her birth was kept quiet until the following year, and when it was finally announced in 1997, it made headlines everywhere. Scientists saw enormous promise for medicine, from studying disease to producing therapeutic proteins. At the same time, the news provoked intense ethical debate, as people asked whether the cloning of humans might follow. Dolly herself lived a relatively ordinary life, produced six lambs, and died in 2003 at the age of six. Preserved by taxidermy, she remains one of the most visited exhibits at the National Museum of Scotland.

vocabulary.definition: any body cell that is not a sperm or egg cell
the part of a female animal that produces milk
a female sheep
moving the nucleus of one cell into another
to cause a strong reaction
connected with what is morally right or wrong
the art of preserving a dead animal's body

C1.title: The lamb that rewrote the rules
C1.text: On this day in 1996, in a shed at the Roslin Institute outside Edinburgh, a lamb was born whose arrival would ripple far beyond the world of animal husbandry. She was called Dolly, and she was the first mammal ever cloned from an adult somatic cell — that is, from an ordinary body cell rather than from the union of egg and sperm. Where every previous mammal had drawn its genes from two parents, Dolly was coaxed into existence from a single cell taken from the mammary gland of a six-year-old ewe, making her that animal's genetic twin. The method, somatic cell nuclear transfer, was as laborious as it was ingenious, yielding a single viable lamb only after 277 attempts. Her creators kept the birth under wraps until February 1997; when the announcement finally came, it detonated across the world's front pages. To researchers, Dolly seemed to open a door onto revolutionary medicine — the study of ageing and disease, the manufacture of therapeutic proteins, perhaps one day the growing of replacement tissues. To many others, she embodied a disquieting new power over life itself, and the phrase "human cloning" was suddenly on every tongue. Dolly lived six years, bred normally, and produced six lambs before her death in 2003. Mounted by taxidermists, she stands today among the most popular exhibits in the National Museum of Scotland — a quiet monument to the moment biology's rules were rewritten.

vocabulary.definition: to spread outward in effects, like waves on water
the farming and breeding of animals
to gently and patiently bring something about
requiring a lot of hard, careful work
to explode; here, to burst dramatically into public view
causing worry or unease

C2.title: Dolly: a genetic twin, born of a single cell
C2.text: On this day in 1996, in an unremarkable shed at the Roslin Institute near Edinburgh, there was born a Finn-Dorset lamb whose existence would unsettle assumptions that had stood since the dawn of biology. Her name was Dolly, and she was the first mammal ever cloned from an adult somatic cell — conjured not from the customary marriage of egg and sperm, but from a solitary cell drawn from the mammary gland of a six-year-old ewe, and thus a genetic replica of an animal already middle-aged. The technique that produced her, somatic cell nuclear transfer, was a feat of extraordinary patience as much as ingenuity: from 277 reconstructed embryos, exactly one grew into a living lamb. Her makers, wary of the storm to come, said nothing until February 1997 — whereupon the announcement duly detonated across the front pages of the world. For the scientific community she was a threshold flung open: onto the study of senescence and disease, the pharmaceutical harvesting of proteins, the distant prospect of regenerating human tissue. For a wider and warier public she was something closer to a portent, and the once-theoretical spectre of human cloning acquired, overnight, an uncomfortable plausibility. Dolly herself, sublimely indifferent to the debate she had ignited, grazed, bred and raised six lambs before dying in 2003, aged six. Preserved by the taxidermist's art, she revolves slowly to this day on her plinth in the National Museum of Scotland — arguably the most consequential farm animal in the history of science.

vocabulary.definition: ordinary; not worth noticing
to bring into being as if by magic
the process of growing old
a sign or warning of something momentous to come
a haunting fear or threatening possibility
the heavy base on which a statue or exhibit stands
